chunks.title: Who Harsh Kaushik is
chunks.text: Harsh Kaushik is a computer science researcher and engineer at Dalhousie University. His work sits at the intersection of trustworthy artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, machine learning, and product engineering. He is based in Halifax, Canada.

chunks.title: Harsh's research interests
chunks.text: Beyond image authenticity, Harsh is interested in intelligent systems that have to earn the user's trust: machine-learning pipelines, multi-agent architectures, secure backends, distributed systems, and interfaces that turn complicated research into useful products. He enjoys the entire journey — from asking the research question and designing the experiment to engineering the system that eventually reaches someone's screen.
aliases: What is Harsh interested in researching?
What kind of systems does he build?

chunks.title: What NexAI is
chunks.text: NexAI is Harsh's flagship research project: An evidence-driven framework for investigating whether an image is human-captured, AI-generated, or manipulated. The research question driving it is: "Can an image be trusted — and what evidence would make that judgment understandable to the person relying on it?" Ongoing research, developed in collaboration with Nextria Inc. through Dalhousie University's NSERC CREATE Cybersecurity Program.
aliases: What does Harsh do with AI images?
Tell me about his image authenticity research.

chunks.title: How NexAI reasons about evidence
chunks.text: NexAI evaluates an image using several categories of evidence: Visual patterns (Low-level and structural signals present in the image itself.); Forensic traces (Artifacts a capture device, an editing tool, or a generative model tends to leave behind.); Metadata (What the file itself claims about its own origin and history.); Provenance (Where an image came from and how it has moved before reaching this decision.); Transformation behaviour (How an image responds to controlled transformations — a signal genuine and generated images don't always share.); Calibrated uncertainty (How confident the framework actually is, and honest about it when it isn't.). Based on that evidence, it reaches one of these decision states: Human-captured, AI-generated, Manipulated, Unknown or uncertain. This overview describes the problem NexAI addresses and how it reasons about evidence — consistent with how the project has been presented publicly. Implementation-specific and model-architecture details are intentionally not published here.
aliases: How does NexAI decide if an image is fake?
What evidence does NexAI look at?

chunks.title: Harsh's technical skills
chunks.text: Frontend: React.js, Next.js, TypeScript. Mobile: React Native, Expo. Styling: Tailwind CSS, Sass, CSS. Backend: Node.js, Express, NestJS, Hono. Database: MongoDB, PostgreSQL. Dev Tools: Git, GitHub, Docker
aliases: What programming languages does he know?

chunks.title: Harsh's featured projects
chunks.text: The two projects Harsh features are NexAI, his image-authenticity research framework, and this portfolio itself — a macOS-inspired interface built from scratch, not a template with a theme applied, with its own window manager, Mission Control, Spotlight search, and Control Center. His full list of public repositories is on GitHub.

chunks.title: How this portfolio itself is engineered
chunks.text: This portfolio is a portfolio interface inspired by desktop operating systems, not a real one. It is built around one shared window manager (every app opens, closes, focuses, drags, resizes, minimizes, and maximizes through the same lifecycle), a Mission Control that shows a live overview of open windows, Control Center preferences that persist appearance and wallpaper choices, Handoff shareable links that restore the exact window and section being shared, an accessible keyboard-driven Spotlight search, a dynamic time-of-day wallpaper, and a responsive architecture where the same window registry powers both the desktop shell and the mobile app grid.
aliases: What is this website made with?
Is this a real operating system?

chunks.title: What Harsh enjoys outside technology
chunks.text: Outside the screen: Harsh photographs skies, streets, and ordinary moments; spends longer in bookstores than planned and prefers physical books; writes poetry and journal entries; collects postcards, coffee mugs, and vinyl records; runs on weekends; and maintains an optimistic relationship with tennis. These interests are not separate from my technical work.
aliases: What does Harsh enjoy outside technology?
What does he do for fun?

chunks.title: Photography
chunks.text: Harsh photographs skies, streets, and ordinary moments that deserve a second look.

chunks.title: Reading
chunks.text: Harsh spends longer in bookstores than planned and prefers physical books.

chunks.title: Writing
chunks.text: Harsh writes poetry and journal entries. As he puts it: "taught me that precision can still contain feeling."

chunks.title: Running
chunks.text: Harsh runs on weekends. As he puts it: "taught me that difficult systems are built one patient mile at a time."

chunks.title: Film
chunks.text: Harsh logs films on Letterboxd (@harshh2001). Recent activity there includes Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man.

chunks.title: Music
chunks.text: Harsh collects vinyl records. He has a public Spotify playlist, "Harsh's Favorite Playlist," described as a collection of songs he keeps coming back to.
aliases: Does he have a Spotify playlist?

chunks.title: Harsh's publications
chunks.text: Harsh has 3 verified publications, listed on his Google Scholar profile: "Design and Development of a Solar-Based Wireless Electric Vehicle Charging System" (2024); "Hybrid Image Processing Device as Wearable Aide for Visually Impaired" (2022); "A Review of Technologies Trending Towards 5G Networks for Smart Healthcare Using IoT" (2022).

chunks.title: Has Harsh given talks?
chunks.text: Harsh has given one featured talk, presented to an industry audience: "From the Research Lab to the Industry Room" at the NSERC Industry Advisory Board Meeting 2026, Dalhousie University, on 2026-06-18.
aliases: What did he present at NSERC?

chunks.title: From the Research Lab to the Industry Room
chunks.text: Harsh presented research developed in collaboration with Nextria Inc. at the NSERC Industry Advisory Board Meeting 2026. The talk, titled "From the Research Lab to the Industry Room," was a three-minute research talk and live demonstration at Dalhousie University on 2026-06-18. It introduced the growing difficulty of determining whether digital images can be trusted, and how forensic and contextual signals can be examined together to support more reliable assessments of image authenticity.
keywords: from the research lab to the industry room
aliases: What was the NSERC talk about?
What did Harsh present at Dalhousie?
